revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Ta-no-Kami]][[en:Ta-no-Kami]][[ja:田の神]][[mni:ꯇ-ꯅꯣ-ꯀꯃꯤ]][[ru:Та-но ками]][[zh:田之神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Ta-no-Kami]][[en:Ta-no-Kami]][[ja:田の神]][[mni:ꯇ-ꯅꯣ-ꯀꯃꯤ]][[ru:Та-но ками]][[zh:田之神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Ta-no-Kami]][[en:Ta-no-Kami]][[ja:田の神]][[mni:ꯇ-ꯅꯣ-ꯀꯃꯤ]][[ru:Та-но ками]][[zh:田之神]] {{Infobox deity|image=File:Tanokansaa Kanoya Nozato.jpg|deity_of=Kami of {{ill|paddy field|WD=Q842623}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q7683716]] Wikidata name: Ta-no-Kami [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Kami believed to observe the harvest of rice plants or to bring a good harvest}} {{more footnotes|date=June 2011}}
{{nihongo|'''Ta-no-Kami'''|田の神}} is a [[kami]] who is believed to observe the harvest of rice plants or to bring a good harvest, by Japanese farmers. ''Ta'' in Japanese means "rice fields". Ta-no-Kami is also called Noushin (kami of agriculture) or kami of peasants. Ta-no-Kami shares the kami of corn, the kami of water and the kami of defense, especially the kami of agriculture associated with mountain faith and {{ill|Veneration of the dead|en|Veneration of the dead|WD=Q238199|lt=Veneration of the dead}} (faith in the [[sorei]]). Ta-no-Kami in {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|en|Kagoshima Prefecture|WD=Q15701|lt=Kagoshima Prefecture}} and parts of {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|WD=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture}} is unique; farmers pray before Ta-no-Kami stone statues in their communities.
==Agricultural kami== In Japan, there are agricultural deities or kami. In the Japanese documents, [[Nihon Shoki]] and [[Kojiki]], there were kami of rice plants, Ukano Mitama, Toyouke Bimeno Kami, and kami of corn, Ootoshino Kami. Of them, Toyouke Bimeno Kami was written also in [[Engishiki]], and is considered to be a female kami. Generally speaking, in the [[:en:Tohoku|Tohoku]] area of Japan, agriculture-related kami is Nougami (agriculture kami), in the [[:en:Koshin|Koshin]] area, it is Saku Gami, in the [[:en:Kinki|Kinki]] area, it is Tsukuri Gami, in the {{ill|Tajima Province|en|Tajima Province|WD=Q1053727|lt=Tajima}} and {{ill|Inaba Province|en|Inaba Province|WD=Q1054338|lt=Inaba}} areas, it is kami of 亥(i),(On the day of i, the fields are struck; which is considered to give peace on the harvest ground). In the [[:en:Chugoku|Chugoku]] and [[Shikoku]] areas, it is Sanbai Sama, in [[:en:Setonaikai|Setonaikai]], it is the local kami. In Eastern Japan it is associated with the Ebisu faith, and in Western Japan it is associated with the [[:en:Daikoku|Daikoku]] faith.
==Festivals or ceremonies== [[File:Rice-transplanting Festival in Katori-jingu 1,katori-city,japan.jpg|right|thumb|Rice transplantation festival at [[Katori Jingu|Katori Shrine]]]] According to their agricultural calendars, farmers observe kami ceremonies related to Ta-no-Kami in the spring and autumn. These include the ceremony of the beginning of a year, beginning of farming in early spring, the start of rice plant farming, rice plant transplantation (accepting kami at the start of transplantation, called Saori) (sending kami at the end is called Sanaburi) and harvest time. They also pray for the elimination of disasters or harmful insects. Finally, they conduct the ceremony of thanking kami for a good harvest, The real ceremonies and their names differ from place to place, although dancing, eating a special dish or rice cakes, or visits to the community kami, and burning ceremonies are some of them. {{ill|Scarecrow|en|Scarecrow|WD=Q1990797|lt=Scarecrow}}s are variations of Ta-no-Kami, since they are expected to prevent bad spirits of animals and birds. Niinamesai is one of the festivals of the Japanese Imperial family, the eating of freshly harvested rice with [[kami]], a variation of the festivals of Ta-no-Kami. Previously it had been a national holiday.
[[File:Kakashi2.jpg|thumb|Scarecrows in a rice paddy]] ==Fox and inari shrine== [[File:Fox0290.jpg|thumb|right|The fox was considered to be the messenger of Ta-no-Kami.]] In many parts of Japan, there are ''Kitsune tsuka'' (small fox shrine); the fox was considered to be a messenger of Ta-no-Kami. {{ill|Inari shrine|en|Inari shrine|WD=Q514480|lt=Inari shrine}}s originated from Kitsune tsuka, and faith in Inari Shrines spread throughout Japan.
==Ta-no-Kami of Kagoshima and Miyazaki Prefectures== In almost all areas of Japan, the real picture of Ta-no-Kami remains unclear, with the single exception of Tanokansaa in {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|en|Kagoshima Prefecture|WD=Q15701|lt=Kagoshima Prefecture}} and parts of {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|WD=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture}}. Tanokansaa is the {{ill|Dialect|en|Dialect|WD=Q33384|lt=Dialect}} of Ta-no-Kami in these prefectures. In their communities, peasants place Tanokansaa stone statues, either of the buddha type and shinto priest type (with or without a mace), or of the peasant type( with or without a pestle). It was started in the 18th century in the [[:en:Satsuma han|Satsuma han]]. In Miyazaki Prefecture, Ta-no-Kami is seen only in areas governed by [[:en:Satsuma han|Satsuma han]]. After the meiji era, this custom spread in accordance with the movements of its inhabitants.
== Folklore == ''Saotome'' (早乙女), or rice planting women, played a religious role in ancient times, rice growing was considered a religious act, and there were many taboos that had to be observed.<ref>{{cite web | url=http://web-japan.org/kidsweb/explore/calendar/june/rice.html | title=Rice Planting - Calendar 06 - Explore Japan - Kids Web Japan - Web Japan }}</ref> The ''inadama'' was the spirit of the rice plant. Since World War 2 rice planting has become mechanized.
===Popular culture=== In the popular ''anime'' series, [[:en:Saotome Ranma|Saotome Ranma]] is the central character in ''{{ill|Ranma ½|en|Ranma ½|WD=Q272015|lt=Ranma ½}}''.
== See also == * {{ill|Polytheism|en|Polytheism|WD=Q9163|lt=Polytheism}}
==References== {{Reflist}} * Ono ''Illustrated Forelore Encyclopedia, 100 figures of Tano Kami'' 1970 yamakawa publishing company *Mikio Aoyama ''Tano Kamis in Miyazaki Prefecture'' 1979, Kohmyaku Sousho, Miyazaki. * Yukawa Y,(1999) ''What is Yamano Kami and Tano Kami'' in ''Encyclopedia of Forelore'' Nihon Jitsugyou Shuppansha, p90-91, {{ISBN|4-534-02985-3}} * Inoguchi S.(1959) ''Nihon Minzokugaku Taikei 7, Seikatsu to Minzoku (2)'' Heibonsha. * Iketsuchi H.(1959) ''Spirits and Kami, how they appear in agriculture Nihon Minzokugaku Taikei, 8, Shikou to Minzoku'' Heibonsha. * Yanagida K. (1951) ''Minzokugaku Jiten'' Tokyodo.{{ISBN|4-490-10001-9}} * Ono S. (1987) ''Tano Kami Demonstration Illustrated'' ''Various types of Tano Kami'' Kagoshima Prefecture Reimeikan.
==External links== * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110930061918/http://www41.tok2.com/home/kanihei5/tanokansa.html Tanokansaa with pictures] * [http://www.isinohotoke.net/tanokansa.htm Tano Kami Pictures (Japanese text)] * [http://www.city.gero.lg.jp/kankou/view.rbz?cd=302 Tano Kami Festival] * [http://www.ebino-kankou.com/tanokansa/index.html What is Tanokami in Japanese]
{{Authority control}}]]

After the meiji era, this custom spread in accordance with the movements of its inhabitants.
== Folklore == ''Saotome'' (早乙女), or rice planting women, played a religious role in ancient times, rice growing was considered a religious act, and there were many taboos that had to be observed.<ref>{{cite web | url=http://web-japan.org/kidsweb/explore/calendar/june/rice.html | title=Rice Planting - Calendar 06 - Explore Japan - Kids Web Japan - Web Japan }}</ref> The ''inadama'' was the spirit of the rice plant. Since World War 2 rice planting has become mechanized.
===Popular culture=== In the popular ''anime'' series, [[:en:Saotome Ranma|Saotome Ranma]] is the central character in ''{{ill|Ranma ½|en|Ranma ½|WD=Q272015|lt=Ranma ½}}''.
== See also == * {{ill|Polytheism|en|Polytheism|WD=Q9163|lt=Polytheism}}

(1951) ''Minzokugaku Jiten'' Tokyodo.{{ISBN|4-490-10001-9}} * Ono S. (1987) ''Tano Kami Demonstration Illustrated'' ''Various types of Tano Kami'' Kagoshima Prefecture Reimeikan.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Ta-no-Kami]][[en:Ta-no-Kami]][[ja:田の神]][[mni:ꯇ-ꯅꯣ-ꯀꯃꯤ]][[ru:Та-но ками]][[zh:田之神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Ta-no-Kami]][[en:Ta-no-Kami]][[ja:田の神]][[mni:ꯇ-ꯅꯣ-ꯀꯃꯤ]][[ru:Та-но ками]][[zh:田之神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Ta-no-Kami]][[en:Ta-no-Kami]][[ja:田の神]][[mni:ꯇ-ꯅꯣ-ꯀꯃꯤ]][[ru:Та-но ками]][[zh:田之神]] {{Infobox deity|image=File:Tanokansaa Kanoya Nozato.jpg|deity_of=Kami of {{ill|paddy field|WD=Q842623}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q7683716]] Wikidata name: Ta-no-Kami [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Kami believed to observe the harvest of rice plants or to bring a good harvest}} {{more footnotes|date=June 2011}}
{{nihongo|'''Ta-no-Kami'''|田の神}} is a [[kami]] who is believed to observe the harvest of rice plants or to bring a good harvest, by Japanese farmers. ''Ta'' in Japanese means "rice fields". Ta-no-Kami is also called Noushin (kami of agriculture) or kami of peasants. Ta-no-Kami shares the kami of corn, the kami of water and the kami of defense, especially the kami of agriculture associated with mountain faith and {{ill|Veneration of the dead|en|Veneration of the dead|WD=Q238199|lt=Veneration of the dead}} (faith in the [[sorei]]). Ta-no-Kami in {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|en|Kagoshima Prefecture|WD=Q15701|lt=Kagoshima Prefecture}} and parts of {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|WD=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture}} is unique; farmers pray before Ta-no-Kami stone statues in their communities.
==Agricultural kami== In Japan, there are agricultural deities or kami. In the Japanese documents, [[Nihon Shoki]] and [[Kojiki]], there were kami of rice plants, Ukano Mitama, Toyouke Bimeno Kami, and kami of corn, Ootoshino Kami. Of them, Toyouke Bimeno Kami was written also in [[Engishiki]], and is considered to be a female kami. Generally speaking, in the [[:en:Tohoku|Tohoku]] area of Japan, agriculture-related kami is Nougami (agriculture kami), in the [[:en:Koshin|Koshin]] area, it is Saku Gami, in the [[:en:Kinki|Kinki]] area, it is Tsukuri Gami, in the {{ill|Tajima Province|en|Tajima Province|WD=Q1053727|lt=Tajima}} and {{ill|Inaba Province|en|Inaba Province|WD=Q1054338|lt=Inaba}} areas, it is kami of 亥(i),(On the day of i, the fields are struck; which is considered to give peace on the harvest ground). In the [[:en:Chugoku|Chugoku]] and [[Shikoku]] areas, it is Sanbai Sama, in [[:en:Setonaikai|Setonaikai]], it is the local kami. In Eastern Japan it is associated with the Ebisu faith, and in Western Japan it is associated with the [[:en:Daikoku|Daikoku]] faith.
==Festivals or ceremonies== [[File:Rice-transplanting Festival in Katori-jingu 1,katori-city,japan.jpg|right|thumb|Rice transplantation festival at [[Katori Jingū|Katori Shrine]]]] According to their agricultural calendars, farmers observe kami ceremonies related to Ta-no-Kami in the spring and autumn. These include the ceremony of the beginning of a year, beginning of farming in early spring, the start of rice plant farming, rice plant transplantation (accepting kami at the start of transplantation, called Saori) (sending kami at the end is called Sanaburi) and harvest time. They also pray for the elimination of disasters or harmful insects. Finally, they conduct the ceremony of thanking kami for a good harvest, The real ceremonies and their names differ from place to place, although dancing, eating a special dish or rice cakes, or visits to the community kami, and burning ceremonies are some of them. {{ill|Scarecrow|en|Scarecrow|WD=Q1990797|lt=Scarecrow}}s are variations of Ta-no-Kami, since they are expected to prevent bad spirits of animals and birds. Niinamesai is one of the festivals of the Japanese Imperial family, the eating of freshly harvested rice with [[kami]], a variation of the festivals of Ta-no-Kami. Previously it had been a national holiday.
[[File:Kakashi2.jpg|thumb|Scarecrows in a rice paddy]] ==Fox and inari shrine== [[File:Fox0290.jpg|thumb|right|The fox was considered to be the messenger of Ta-no-Kami.]] In many parts of Japan, there are ''Kitsune tsuka'' (small fox shrine); the fox was considered to be a messenger of Ta-no-Kami. {{ill|Inari shrine|en|Inari shrine|WD=Q514480|lt=Inari shrine}}s originated from Kitsune tsuka, and faith in Inari Shrines spread throughout Japan.
==Ta-no-Kami of Kagoshima and Miyazaki Prefectures== In almost all areas of Japan, the real picture of Ta-no-Kami remains unclear, with the single exception of Tanokansaa in {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|en|Kagoshima Prefecture|WD=Q15701|lt=Kagoshima Prefecture}} and parts of {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|WD=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture}}. Tanokansaa is the {{ill|Dialect|en|Dialect|WD=Q33384|lt=Dialect}} of Ta-no-Kami in these prefectures. In their communities, peasants place Tanokansaa stone statues, either of the buddha type and shinto priest type (with or without a mace), or of the peasant type( with or without a pestle). It was started in the 18th century in the [[:en:Satsuma han|Satsuma han]]. In Miyazaki Prefecture, Ta-no-Kami is seen only in areas governed by [[:en:Satsuma han|Satsuma han]]. After the meiji era, this custom spread in accordance with the movements of its inhabitants.
== Folklore == ''Saotome'' (早乙女), or rice planting women, played a religious role in ancient times, rice growing was considered a religious act, and there were many taboos that had to be observed.<ref>{{cite web | url=http://web-japan.org/kidsweb/explore/calendar/june/rice.html | title=Rice Planting - Calendar 06 - Explore Japan - Kids Web Japan - Web Japan }}</ref> The ''inadama'' was the spirit of the rice plant. Since World War 2 rice planting has become mechanized.
===Popular culture=== In the popular ''anime'' series, [[:en:Saotome Ranma|Saotome Ranma]] is the central character in ''{{ill|Ranma ½|en|Ranma ½|WD=Q272015|lt=Ranma ½}}''.
== See also == * {{ill|Polytheism|en|Polytheism|WD=Q9163|lt=Polytheism}}
==References== {{Reflist}} * Ono ''Illustrated Forelore Encyclopedia, 100 figures of Tano Kami'' 1970 yamakawa publishing company *Mikio Aoyama ''Tano Kamis in Miyazaki Prefecture'' 1979, Kohmyaku Sousho, Miyazaki. * Yukawa Y,(1999) ''What is Yamano Kami and Tano Kami'' in ''Encyclopedia of Forelore'' Nihon Jitsugyou Shuppansha, p90-91, {{ISBN|4-534-02985-3}} * Inoguchi S.(1959) ''Nihon Minzokugaku Taikei 7, Seikatsu to Minzoku (2)'' Heibonsha. * Iketsuchi H.(1959) ''Spirits and Kami, how they appear in agriculture Nihon Minzokugaku Taikei, 8, Shikou to Minzoku'' Heibonsha. * Yanagida K. (1951) ''Minzokugaku Jiten'' Tokyodo.{{ISBN|4-490-10001-9}} * Ono S. (1987) ''Tano Kami Demonstration Illustrated'' ''Various types of Tano Kami'' Kagoshima Prefecture Reimeikan.
==External links== * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110930061918/http://www41.tok2.com/home/kanihei5/tanokansa.html Tanokansaa with pictures] * [http://www.isinohotoke.net/tanokansa.htm Tano Kami Pictures (Japanese text)] * [http://www.city.gero.lg.jp/kankou/view.rbz?cd=302 Tano Kami Festival] * [http://www.ebino-kankou.com/tanokansa/index.html What is Tanokami in Japanese]
{{Authority control}}]]

It was started in the 18th century in the [[:en:Satsuma han|Satsuma han]]. In Miyazaki Prefecture, Ta-no-Kami is seen only in areas governed by [[:en:Satsuma han|Satsuma han]]. After the meiji era, this custom spread in accordance with the movements of its inhabitants.
== Folklore == ''Saotome'' (早乙女), or rice planting women, played a religious role in ancient times, rice growing was considered a religious act, and there were many taboos that had to be observed.<ref>{{cite web | url=http://web-japan.org/kidsweb/explore/calendar/june/rice.html | title=Rice Planting - Calendar 06 - Explore Japan - Kids Web Japan - Web Japan }}</ref> The ''inadama'' was the spirit of the rice plant. Since World War 2 rice planting has become mechanized.
===Popular culture=== In the popular ''anime'' series, [[:en:Saotome Ranma|Saotome Ranma]] is the central character in ''{{ill|Ranma ½|en|Ranma ½|WD=Q272015|lt=Ranma ½}}''.
== See also == * {{ill|Polytheism|en|Polytheism|WD=Q9163|lt=Polytheism}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Ta-no-Kami]][[en:Ta-no-Kami]][[ja:田の神]][[mni:ꯇ-ꯅꯣ-ꯀꯃꯤ]][[ru:Та-но ками]][[zh:田之神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Ta-no-Kami]][[en:Ta-no-Kami]][[ja:田の神]][[mni:ꯇ-ꯅꯣ-ꯀꯃꯤ]][[ru:Та-но ками]][[zh:田之神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Ta-no-Kami]][[en:Ta-no-Kami]][[ja:田の神]][[mni:ꯇ-ꯅꯣ-ꯀꯃꯤ]][[ru:Та-но ками]][[zh:田之神]] {{Infobox deity|image=File:Tanokansaa Kanoya Nozato.jpg|deity_of=Kami of {{ill|paddy field|WD=Q842623}}}} {{Short description|Kami believed to observe the harvest of rice plants or to bring a good harvest}} {{more footnotes|date=June 2011}}
{{nihongo|'''Ta-no-Kami'''|田の神}} is a [[kami]] who is believed to observe the harvest of rice plants or to bring a good harvest, by Japanese farmers. ''Ta'' in Japanese means "rice fields". Ta-no-Kami is also called Noushin (kami of agriculture) or kami of peasants. Ta-no-Kami shares the kami of corn, the kami of water and the kami of defense, especially the kami of agriculture associated with mountain faith and {{ill|Veneration of the dead|en|Veneration of the dead|WD=Q238199|lt=Veneration of the dead}} (faith in the [[sorei]]). Ta-no-Kami in {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|en|Kagoshima Prefecture|WD=Q15701|lt=Kagoshima Prefecture}} and parts of {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|WD=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture}} is unique; farmers pray before Ta-no-Kami stone statues in their communities.
==Agricultural kami== In Japan, there are agricultural deities or kami. In the Japanese documents, [[Nihon Shoki]] and [[Kojiki]], there were kami of rice plants, Ukano Mitama, Toyouke Bimeno Kami, and kami of corn, Ootoshino Kami. Of them, Toyouke Bimeno Kami was written also in [[Engishiki]], and is considered to be a female kami. Generally speaking, in the [[:en:Tohoku|Tohoku]] area of Japan, agriculture-related kami is Nougami (agriculture kami), in the [[:en:Koshin|Koshin]] area, it is Saku Gami, in the [[:en:Kinki|Kinki]] area, it is Tsukuri Gami, in the {{ill|Tajima Province|en|Tajima Province|WD=Q1053727|lt=Tajima}} and {{ill|Inaba Province|en|Inaba Province|WD=Q1054338|lt=Inaba}} areas, it is kami of 亥(i),(On the day of i, the fields are struck; which is considered to give peace on the harvest ground). In the [[:en:Chugoku|Chugoku]] and [[Shikoku]] areas, it is Sanbai Sama, in [[:en:Setonaikai|Setonaikai]], it is the local kami. In Eastern Japan it is associated with the Ebisu faith, and in Western Japan it is associated with the [[:en:Daikoku|Daikoku]] faith.
==Festivals or ceremonies== [[File:Rice-transplanting Festival in Katori-jingu 1,katori-city,japan.jpg|right|thumb|Rice transplantation festival at [[Katori Jingū|Katori Shrine]]]] According to their agricultural calendars, farmers observe kami ceremonies related to Ta-no-Kami in the spring and autumn. These include the ceremony of the beginning of a year, beginning of farming in early spring, the start of rice plant farming, rice plant transplantation (accepting kami at the start of transplantation, called Saori) (sending kami at the end is called Sanaburi) and harvest time. They also pray for the elimination of disasters or harmful insects. Finally, they conduct the ceremony of thanking kami for a good harvest, The real ceremonies and their names differ from place to place, although dancing, eating a special dish or rice cakes, or visits to the community kami, and burning ceremonies are some of them. {{ill|Scarecrow|en|Scarecrow|WD=Q1990797|lt=Scarecrow}}s are variations of Ta-no-Kami, since they are expected to prevent bad spirits of animals and birds. Niinamesai is one of the festivals of the Japanese Imperial family, the eating of freshly harvested rice with [[kami]], a variation of the festivals of Ta-no-Kami. Previously it had been a national holiday.
[[File:Kakashi2.jpg|thumb|Scarecrows in a rice paddy]] ==Fox and inari shrine== [[File:Fox0290.jpg|thumb|right|The fox was considered to be the messenger of Ta-no-Kami.]] In many parts of Japan, there are ''Kitsune tsuka'' (small fox shrine); the fox was considered to be a messenger of Ta-no-Kami. {{ill|Inari shrine|en|Inari shrine|WD=Q514480|lt=Inari shrine}}s originated from Kitsune tsuka, and faith in Inari Shrines spread throughout Japan.
==Ta-no-Kami of Kagoshima and Miyazaki Prefectures== In almost all areas of Japan, the real picture of Ta-no-Kami remains unclear, with the single exception of Tanokansaa in {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|en|Kagoshima Prefecture|WD=Q15701|lt=Kagoshima Prefecture}} and parts of {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|WD=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture}}. Tanokansaa is the {{ill|Dialect|en|Dialect|WD=Q33384|lt=Dialect}} of Ta-no-Kami in these prefectures. In their communities, peasants place Tanokansaa stone statues, either of the buddha type and shinto priest type (with or without a mace), or of the peasant type( with or without a pestle). It was started in the 18th century in the [[:en:Satsuma han|Satsuma han]]. In Miyazaki Prefecture, Ta-no-Kami is seen only in areas governed by [[:en:Satsuma han|Satsuma han]]. After the meiji era, this custom spread in accordance with the movements of its inhabitants.
== Folklore == ''Saotome'' (早乙女), or rice planting women, played a religious role in ancient times, rice growing was considered a religious act, and there were many taboos that had to be observed.<ref>{{cite web | url=http://web-japan.org/kidsweb/explore/calendar/june/rice.html | title=Rice Planting - Calendar 06 - Explore Japan - Kids Web Japan - Web Japan }}</ref> The ''inadama'' was the spirit of the rice plant. Since World War 2 rice planting has become mechanized.
===Popular culture=== In the popular ''anime'' series, [[:en:Saotome Ranma|Saotome Ranma]] is the central character in ''{{ill|Ranma ½|en|Ranma ½|WD=Q272015|lt=Ranma ½}}''.
== See also == * {{ill|Polytheism|en|Polytheism|WD=Q9163|lt=Polytheism}}
==References== {{Reflist}} * Ono ''Illustrated Forelore Encyclopedia, 100 figures of Tano Kami'' 1970 yamakawa publishing company *Mikio Aoyama ''Tano Kamis in Miyazaki Prefecture'' 1979, Kohmyaku Sousho, Miyazaki. * Yukawa Y,(1999) ''What is Yamano Kami and Tano Kami'' in ''Encyclopedia of Forelore'' Nihon Jitsugyou Shuppansha, p90-91, {{ISBN|4-534-02985-3}} * Inoguchi S.(1959) ''Nihon Minzokugaku Taikei 7, Seikatsu to Minzoku (2)'' Heibonsha. * Iketsuchi H.(1959) ''Spirits and Kami, how they appear in agriculture Nihon Minzokugaku Taikei, 8, Shikou to Minzoku'' Heibonsha. * Yanagida K. (1951) ''Minzokugaku Jiten'' Tokyodo.{{ISBN|4-490-10001-9}} * Ono S. (1987) ''Tano Kami Demonstration Illustrated'' ''Various types of Tano Kami'' Kagoshima Prefecture Reimeikan.
==External links== * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110930061918/http://www41.tok2.com/home/kanihei5/tanokansa.html Tanokansaa with pictures] * [http://www.isinohotoke.net/tanokansa.htm Tano Kami Pictures (Japanese text)] * [http://www.city.gero.lg.jp/kankou/view.rbz?cd=302 Tano Kami Festival] * [http://www.ebino-kankou.com/tanokansa/index.html What is Tanokami in Japanese]
{{Authority control}}]]

== Folklore == ''Saotome'' (早乙女), or rice planting women, played a religious role in ancient times, rice growing was considered a religious act, and there were many taboos that had to be observed.<ref>{{cite web | url=http://web-japan.org/kidsweb/explore/calendar/june/rice.html | title=Rice Planting - Calendar 06 - Explore Japan - Kids Web Japan - Web Japan }}</ref> The ''inadama'' was the spirit of the rice plant. Since World War 2 rice planting has become mechanized.
===Popular culture=== In the popular ''anime'' series, [[:en:Saotome Ranma|Saotome Ranma]] is the central character in ''{{ill|Ranma ½|en|Ranma ½|WD=Q272015|lt=Ranma ½|1=Ranma ½}}''.
== See also == * {{ill|Polytheism|en|Polytheism|WD=Q9163|lt=Polytheism|1=Polytheism}}
==References== {{Reflist}} * Ono ''Illustrated Forelore Encyclopedia, 100 figures of Tano Kami'' 1970 yamakawa publishing company *Mikio Aoyama ''Tano Kamis in Miyazaki Prefecture'' 1979, Kohmyaku Sousho, Miyazaki. * Yukawa Y,(1999) ''What is Yamano Kami and Tano Kami'' in ''Encyclopedia of Forelore'' Nihon Jitsugyou Shuppansha, p90-91, {{ISBN|4-534-02985-3}} * Inoguchi S.(1959) ''Nihon Minzokugaku Taikei 7, Seikatsu to Minzoku (2)'' Heibonsha. * Iketsuchi H.(1959) ''Spirits and Kami, how they appear in agriculture Nihon Minzokugaku Taikei, 8, Shikou to Minzoku'' Heibonsha. * Yanagida K. (1951) ''Minzokugaku Jiten'' Tokyodo.{{ISBN|4-490-10001-9}} * Ono S. (1987) ''Tano Kami Demonstration Illustrated'' ''Various types of Tano Kami'' Kagoshima Prefecture Reimeikan.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Ta-no-Kami]][[en:Ta-no-Kami]][[ja:田の神]][[mni:ꯇ-ꯅꯣ-ꯀꯃꯤ]][[ru:Та-но ками]][[zh:田之神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Ta-no-Kami]][[en:Ta-no-Kami]][[ja:田の神]][[mni:ꯇ-ꯅꯣ-ꯀꯃꯤ]][[ru:Та-но ками]][[zh:田之神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Ta-no-Kami]][[en:Ta-no-Kami]][[ja:田の神]][[mni:ꯇ-ꯅꯣ-ꯀꯃꯤ]][[ru:Та-но ками]][[zh:田之神]] {{Infobox deity|image=File:Tanokansaa Kanoya Nozato.jpg|deity_of=Kami of {{ill|paddy field|WD=Q842623|1=Paddy field}}}} {{Short description|Kami believed to observe the harvest of rice plants or to bring a good harvest}} {{more footnotes|date=June 2011}}
{{nihongo|'''Ta-no-Kami'''|田の神}} is a [[kami]] who is believed to observe the harvest of rice plants or to bring a good harvest, by Japanese farmers. ''Ta'' in Japanese means "rice fields". Ta-no-Kami is also called Noushin (kami of agriculture) or kami of peasants. Ta-no-Kami shares the kami of corn, the kami of water and the kami of defense, especially the kami of agriculture associated with mountain faith and {{ill|Veneration of the dead|en|Veneration of the dead|WD=Q238199|lt=Veneration of the dead|1=Veneration of the dead}} (faith in the [[sorei]]). Ta-no-Kami in {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|en|Kagoshima Prefecture|WD=Q15701|lt=Kagoshima Prefecture|1=Kagoshima Prefecture}} and parts of {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|WD=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture|1=Miyazaki Prefecture}} is unique; farmers pray before Ta-no-Kami stone statues in their communities.
==Agricultural kami== In Japan, there are agricultural deities or kami. In the Japanese documents, [[Nihon Shoki]] and [[Kojiki]], there were kami of rice plants, Ukano Mitama, Toyouke Bimeno Kami, and kami of corn, Ootoshino Kami. Of them, Toyouke Bimeno Kami was written also in [[Engishiki]], and is considered to be a female kami. Generally speaking, in the [[:en:Tohoku|Tohoku]] area of Japan, agriculture-related kami is Nougami (agriculture kami), in the [[:en:Koshin|Koshin]] area, it is Saku Gami, in the [[:en:Kinki|Kinki]] area, it is Tsukuri Gami, in the {{ill|Tajima Province|en|Tajima Province|WD=Q1053727|lt=Tajima|1=Tajima Province}} and {{ill|Inaba Province|en|Inaba Province|WD=Q1054338|lt=Inaba|1=Inaba Province}} areas, it is kami of 亥(i),(On the day of i, the fields are struck; which is considered to give peace on the harvest ground). In the [[:en:Chugoku|Chugoku]] and [[Shikoku]] areas, it is Sanbai Sama, in [[:en:Setonaikai|Setonaikai]], it is the local kami. In Eastern Japan it is associated with the Ebisu faith, and in Western Japan it is associated with the [[:en:Daikoku|Daikoku]] faith.
==Festivals or ceremonies== [[File:Rice-transplanting Festival in Katori-jingu 1,katori-city,japan.jpg|right|thumb|Rice transplantation festival at [[Katori Jingū|Katori Shrine]]]] According to their agricultural calendars, farmers observe kami ceremonies related to Ta-no-Kami in the spring and autumn. These include the ceremony of the beginning of a year, beginning of farming in early spring, the start of rice plant farming, rice plant transplantation (accepting kami at the start of transplantation, called Saori) (sending kami at the end is called Sanaburi) and harvest time. They also pray for the elimination of disasters or harmful insects. Finally, they conduct the ceremony of thanking kami for a good harvest, The real ceremonies and their names differ from place to place, although dancing, eating a special dish or rice cakes, or visits to the community kami, and burning ceremonies are some of them. {{ill|Scarecrow|en|Scarecrow|WD=Q1990797|lt=Scarecrow|1=Scarecrow}}s are variations of Ta-no-Kami, since they are expected to prevent bad spirits of animals and birds. Niinamesai is one of the festivals of the Japanese Imperial family, the eating of freshly harvested rice with [[kami]], a variation of the festivals of Ta-no-Kami. Previously it had been a national holiday.
[[File:Kakashi2.jpg|thumb|Scarecrows in a rice paddy]] ==Fox and inari shrine== [[File:Fox0290.jpg|thumb|right|The fox was considered to be the messenger of Ta-no-Kami.]] In many parts of Japan, there are ''Kitsune tsuka'' (small fox shrine); the fox was considered to be a messenger of Ta-no-Kami. {{ill|Inari shrine|en|Inari shrine|WD=Q514480|lt=Inari shrine|1=Inari shrine}}s originated from Kitsune tsuka, and faith in Inari Shrines spread throughout Japan.
==Ta-no-Kami of Kagoshima and Miyazaki Prefectures== In almost all areas of Japan, the real picture of Ta-no-Kami remains unclear, with the single exception of Tanokansaa in {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|en|Kagoshima Prefecture|WD=Q15701|lt=Kagoshima Prefecture|1=Kagoshima Prefecture}} and parts of {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|WD=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture|1=Miyazaki Prefecture}}. Tanokansaa is the {{ill|Dialect|en|Dialect|WD=Q33384|lt=Dialect|1=Dialect}} of Ta-no-Kami in these prefectures. In their communities, peasants place Tanokansaa stone statues, either of the buddha type and shinto priest type (with or without a mace), or of the peasant type( with or without a pestle). It was started in the 18th century in the [[:en:Satsuma han|Satsuma han]]. In Miyazaki Prefecture, Ta-no-Kami is seen only in areas governed by [[:en:Satsuma han|Satsuma han]]. After the meiji era, this custom spread in accordance with the movements of its inhabitants.
== Folklore == ''Saotome'' (早乙女), or rice planting women, played a religious role in ancient times, rice growing was considered a religious act, and there were many taboos that had to be observed.<ref>{{cite web | url=http://web-japan.org/kidsweb/explore/calendar/june/rice.html | title=Rice Planting - Calendar 06 - Explore Japan - Kids Web Japan - Web Japan }}</ref> The ''inadama'' was the spirit of the rice plant. Since World War 2 rice planting has become mechanized.
===Popular culture=== In the popular ''anime'' series, [[:en:Saotome Ranma|Saotome Ranma]] is the central character in ''{{ill|Ranma ½|en|Ranma ½|WD=Q272015|lt=Ranma ½|1=Ranma ½}}''.
== See also == * {{ill|Polytheism|en|Polytheism|WD=Q9163|lt=Polytheism|1=Polytheism}}
==References== {{Reflist}} * Ono ''Illustrated Forelore Encyclopedia, 100 figures of Tano Kami'' 1970 yamakawa publishing company *Mikio Aoyama ''Tano Kamis in Miyazaki Prefecture'' 1979, Kohmyaku Sousho, Miyazaki. * Yukawa Y,(1999) ''What is Yamano Kami and Tano Kami'' in ''Encyclopedia of Forelore'' Nihon Jitsugyou Shuppansha, p90-91, {{ISBN|4-534-02985-3}} * Inoguchi S.(1959) ''Nihon Minzokugaku Taikei 7, Seikatsu to Minzoku (2)'' Heibonsha. * Iketsuchi H.(1959) ''Spirits and Kami, how they appear in agriculture Nihon Minzokugaku Taikei, 8, Shikou to Minzoku'' Heibonsha. * Yanagida K. (1951) ''Minzokugaku Jiten'' Tokyodo.{{ISBN|4-490-10001-9}} * Ono S. (1987) ''Tano Kami Demonstration Illustrated'' ''Various types of Tano Kami'' Kagoshima Prefecture Reimeikan.
==External links== * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110930061918/http://www41.tok2.com/home/kanihei5/tanokansa.html Tanokansaa with pictures] * [http://www.isinohotoke.net/tanokansa.htm Tano Kami Pictures (Japanese text)] * [http://www.city.gero.lg.jp/kankou/view.rbz?cd=302 Tano Kami Festival] * [http://www.ebino-kankou.com/tanokansa/index.html What is Tanokami in Japanese]
{{Authority control}}]]

Tanokansaa is the {{ill|Dialect|en|Dialect|WD=Q33384|lt=Dialect|1=Dialect}} of Ta-no-Kami in these prefectures. In their communities, peasants place Tanokansaa stone statues, either of the buddha type and shinto priest type (with or without a mace), or of the peasant type( with or without a pestle). It was started in the 18th century in the [[:en:Satsuma han|Satsuma han]]. In Miyazaki Prefecture, Ta-no-Kami is seen only in areas governed by [[:en:Satsuma han|Satsuma han]]. After the meiji era, this custom spread in accordance with the movements of its inhabitants.
== Folklore == ''Saotome'' (早乙女), or rice planting women, played a religious role in ancient times, rice growing was considered a religious act, and there were many taboos that had to be observed.<ref>{{cite web | url=http://web-japan.org/kidsweb/explore/calendar/june/rice.html | title=Rice Planting - Calendar 06 - Explore Japan - Kids Web Japan - Web Japan }}</ref> The ''inadama'' was the spirit of the rice plant. Since World War 2 rice planting has become mechanized.
===Popular culture=== In the popular ''anime'' series, [[:en:Saotome Ranma|Saotome Ranma]] is the central character in ''{{ill|Ranma ½|en|Ranma ½|WD=Q272015|lt=Ranma ½|1=Ranma ½}}''.
== See also == * {{ill|Polytheism|en|Polytheism|WD=Q9163|lt=Polytheism|1=Polytheism}}
==References== {{Reflist}} * Ono ''Illustrated Forelore Encyclopedia, 100 figures of Tano Kami'' 1970 yamakawa publishing company *Mikio Aoyama ''Tano Kamis in Miyazaki Prefecture'' 1979, Kohmyaku Sousho, Miyazaki. * Yukawa Y,(1999) ''What is Yamano Kami and Tano Kami'' in ''Encyclopedia of Forelore'' Nihon Jitsugyou Shuppansha, p90-91, {{ISBN|4-534-02985-3}} * Inoguchi S.(1959) ''Nihon Minzokugaku Taikei 7, Seikatsu to Minzoku (2)'' Heibonsha. * Iketsuchi H.(1959) ''Spirits and Kami, how they appear in agriculture Nihon Minzokugaku Taikei, 8, Shikou to Minzoku'' Heibonsha. * Yanagida K. (1951) ''Minzokugaku Jiten'' Tokyodo.{{ISBN|4-490-10001-9}} * Ono S. (1987) ''Tano Kami Demonstration Illustrated'' ''Various types of Tano Kami'' Kagoshima Prefecture Reimeikan.
==External links== * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110930061918/http://www41.tok2.com/home/kanihei5/tanokansa.html Tanokansaa with pictures] * [http://www.isinohotoke.net/tanokansa.htm Tano Kami Pictures (Japanese text)] * [http://www.city.gero.lg.jp/kankou/view.rbz?cd=302 Tano Kami Festival] * [http://www.ebino-kankou.com/tanokansa/index.html What is Tanokami in Japanese]
{{Authority control}}]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Ta-no-Kami]][[en:Ta-no-Kami]][[ja:田の神]][[mni:ꯇ-ꯅꯣ-ꯀꯃꯤ]][[ru:Та-но ками]][[zh:田之神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Ta-no-Kami]][[en:Ta-no-Kami]][[ja:田の神]][[mni:ꯇ-ꯅꯣ-ꯀꯃꯤ]][[ru:Та-но ками]][[zh:田之神]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Ta-no-Kami]][[en:Ta-no-Kami]][[ja:田の神]][[mni:ꯇ-ꯅꯣ-ꯀꯃꯤ]][[ru:Та-но ками]][[zh:田之神]] {{Infobox deity|image=File:Tanokansaa Kanoya Nozato.jpg|deity_of=Kami of {{ill|paddy field|WD=Q842623|1=Paddy field}}}} {{Short description|Kami believed to observe the harvest of rice plants or to bring a good harvest}} {{more footnotes|date=June 2011}}
{{nihongo|'''Ta-no-Kami'''|田の神}} is a [[kami]] who is believed to observe the harvest of rice plants or to bring a good harvest, by Japanese farmers. ''Ta'' in Japanese means "rice fields". Ta-no-Kami is also called Noushin (kami of agriculture) or kami of peasants. Ta-no-Kami shares the kami of corn, the kami of water and the kami of defense, especially the kami of agriculture associated with mountain faith and {{ill|Veneration of the dead|en|Veneration of the dead|WD=Q238199|lt=Veneration of the dead|1=Veneration of the dead}} (faith in the [[sorei]]). Ta-no-Kami in {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|en|Kagoshima Prefecture|WD=Q15701|lt=Kagoshima Prefecture|1=Kagoshima Prefecture}} and parts of {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|WD=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture|1=Miyazaki Prefecture}} is unique; farmers pray before Ta-no-Kami stone statues in their communities.
==Agricultural kami== In Japan, there are agricultural deities or kami. In the Japanese documents, [[Nihon Shoki]] and [[Kojiki]], there were kami of rice plants, Ukano Mitama, Toyouke Bimeno Kami, and kami of corn, Ootoshino Kami. Of them, Toyouke Bimeno Kami was written also in [[Engishiki]], and is considered to be a female kami. Generally speaking, in the [[:en:Tohoku|Tohoku]] area of Japan, agriculture-related kami is Nougami (agriculture kami), in the [[:en:Koshin|Koshin]] area, it is Saku Gami, in the [[:en:Kinki|Kinki]] area, it is Tsukuri Gami, in the {{ill|Tajima Province|en|Tajima Province|WD=Q1053727|lt=Tajima|1=Tajima Province}} and {{ill|Inaba Province|en|Inaba Province|WD=Q1054338|lt=Inaba|1=Inaba Province}} areas, it is kami of 亥(i),(On the day of i, the fields are struck; which is considered to give peace on the harvest ground). In the [[:en:Chugoku|Chugoku]] and [[Shikoku]] areas, it is Sanbai Sama, in [[:en:Setonaikai|Setonaikai]], it is the local kami. In Eastern Japan it is associated with the Ebisu faith, and in Western Japan it is associated with the [[:en:Daikoku|Daikoku]] faith.
==Festivals or ceremonies== [[File:Rice-transplanting Festival in Katori-jingu 1,katori-city,japan.jpg|right|thumb|Rice transplantation festival at [[Katori Jingū|Katori Shrine]]]] According to their agricultural calendars, farmers observe kami ceremonies related to Ta-no-Kami in the spring and autumn. These include the ceremony of the beginning of a year, beginning of farming in early spring, the start of rice plant farming, rice plant transplantation (accepting kami at the start of transplantation, called Saori) (sending kami at the end is called Sanaburi) and harvest time. They also pray for the elimination of disasters or harmful insects. Finally, they conduct the ceremony of thanking kami for a good harvest, The real ceremonies and their names differ from place to place, although dancing, eating a special dish or rice cakes, or visits to the community kami, and burning ceremonies are some of them. {{ill|Scarecrow|en|Scarecrow|WD=Q1990797|lt=Scarecrow|1=Scarecrow}}s are variations of Ta-no-Kami, since they are expected to prevent bad spirits of animals and birds. Niinamesai is one of the festivals of the Japanese Imperial family, the eating of freshly harvested rice with [[kami]], a variation of the festivals of Ta-no-Kami. Previously it had been a national holiday.
[[File:Kakashi2.jpg|thumb|Scarecrows in a rice paddy]] ==Fox and inari shrine== [[File:Fox0290.jpg|thumb|right|The fox was considered to be the messenger of Ta-no-Kami.]] In many parts of Japan, there are ''Kitsune tsuka'' (small fox shrine); the fox was considered to be a messenger of Ta-no-Kami. {{ill|Inari shrine|en|Inari shrine|WD=Q514480|lt=Inari shrine|1=Inari shrine}}s originated from Kitsune tsuka, and faith in Inari Shrines spread throughout Japan.
==Ta-no-Kami of Kagoshima and Miyazaki Prefectures== In almost all areas of Japan, the real picture of Ta-no-Kami remains unclear, with the single exception of Tanokansaa in {{ill|Kagoshima Prefecture|en|Kagoshima Prefecture|WD=Q15701|lt=Kagoshima Prefecture|1=Kagoshima Prefecture}} and parts of {{ill|Miyazaki Prefecture|en|Miyazaki Prefecture|WD=Q130300|lt=Miyazaki Prefecture|1=Miyazaki Prefecture}}. Tanokansaa is the {{ill|Dialect|en|Dialect|WD=Q33384|lt=Dialect|1=Dialect}} of Ta-no-Kami in these prefectures. In their communities, peasants place Tanokansaa stone statues, either of the buddha type and shinto priest type (with or without a mace), or of the peasant type( with or without a pestle). It was started in the 18th century in the [[:en:Satsuma han|Satsuma han]]. In Miyazaki Prefecture, Ta-no-Kami is seen only in areas governed by [[:en:Satsuma han|Satsuma han]]. After the meiji era, this custom spread in accordance with the movements of its inhabitants.
== Folklore == ''Saotome'' (早乙女), or rice planting women, played a religious role in ancient times, rice growing was considered a religious act, and there were many taboos that had to be observed.<ref>{{cite web | url=http://web-japan.org/kidsweb/explore/calendar/june/rice.html | title=Rice Planting - Calendar 06 - Explore Japan - Kids Web Japan - Web Japan }}</ref> The ''inadama'' was the spirit of the rice plant. Since World War 2 rice planting has become mechanized.
===Popular culture=== In the popular ''anime'' series, [[:en:Saotome Ranma|Saotome Ranma]] is the central character in ''{{ill|Ranma ½|en|Ranma ½|WD=Q272015|lt=Ranma ½|1=Ranma ½}}''.
== See also == * {{ill|Polytheism|en|Polytheism|WD=Q9163|lt=Polytheism|1=Polytheism}}
==References== {{Reflist}} * Ono ''Illustrated Forelore Encyclopedia, 100 figures of Tano Kami'' 1970 yamakawa publishing company *Mikio Aoyama ''Tano Kamis in Miyazaki Prefecture'' 1979, Kohmyaku Sousho, Miyazaki. * Yukawa Y,(1999) ''What is Yamano Kami and Tano Kami'' in ''Encyclopedia of Forelore'' Nihon Jitsugyou Shuppansha, p90-91, {{ISBN|4-534-02985-3}} * Inoguchi S.(1959) ''Nihon Minzokugaku Taikei 7, Seikatsu to Minzoku (2)'' Heibonsha. * Iketsuchi H.(1959) ''Spirits and Kami, how they appear in agriculture Nihon Minzokugaku Taikei, 8, Shikou to Minzoku'' Heibonsha. * Yanagida K. (1951) ''Minzokugaku Jiten'' Tokyodo.{{ISBN|4-490-10001-9}} * Ono S. (1987) ''Tano Kami Demonstration Illustrated'' ''Various types of Tano Kami'' Kagoshima Prefecture Reimeikan.
==External links== * [https://web.archive.org/web/20110930061918/http://www41.tok2.com/home/kanihei5/tanokansa.html Tanokansaa with pictures] * [http://www.isinohotoke.net/tanokansa.htm Tano Kami Pictures (Japanese text)] * [http://www.city.gero.lg.jp/kankou/view.rbz?cd=302 Tano Kami Festival] * [http://www.ebino-kankou.com/tanokansa/index.html What is Tanokami in Japanese]
{{Authority control}}]]
